title: Home
text:
Welcome to my coursework website for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
This website showcases the visualisations, analysis, and exercises that I have completed throughout the module. It serves as a portfolio of my learning journey in visual analytics using Quarto and R.

title: About
text:
This website was created for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
Its purpose is to document and present the visualisations, analysis, and coursework outputs that I have completed throughout the module. The site acts as a structured portfolio of my work, allowing different exercises and assignments to be organised into separate sections for easy navigation.
The website was built using:

Quarto for website generation
RStudio for development
R for data visualisation and analysis
GitHub for version control
Vercel for deployment

Through this website, I aim to present my coursework in a clear, organised, and accessible format.

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
This exercise looks to explore the fundamentals of visualisations with R.
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_bar
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_dotplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.









Note: y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
The following code below does 2 steps:

scale_y_continuous is used to turn off the y-axis, and
binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_histogram()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









It is to note that the default bin is 30.

Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
The following code chunk does the following:

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  










Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
The following code changes the fill of the histogram by using sub-group of `aesthetic()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_density()
This computes and plots the kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useflu alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








The next code plots 2 kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_boxplot()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








Notches are used in boxplots to help visually assess whether medians of distributions differ. If it does not overlap, it means that the medians are different.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_violin

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_point()
This is to create scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Combinatiation of geom objects
The code below shows the plot of data points on the boxplot by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
stat_summary()
This adds mean values by using stat_summary() and overrides the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom()
This adds mean values by using geom_() and overrides the default stats.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
text:
Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
The code below shows geom_smooth() being used to plot a best-fit curve in the scatterplot. The default method used is loess

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_wrap()
This code wraps a 1-D sequence of panels into 2-D. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_grid()
This code forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Working with Coordinates

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
coord_flip()
Flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Changing the range of x & y-axis
text:
Changing the range of x & y-axis
As the scatterplot below is slightly misleading due to the axis range not being equal,

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









the code below fixes both axis range from 0 to 100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

text:
This section contains the in-class exercises completed for the module.

Exercise 1

title: Hands-On Exercise 2
text:
This exercise builds on what was covered in Hands-On Exercise 1, where we explore more extensions of ggplot2 extensions that will allow the creation of a more elegant and effective statistical graphics.
In this exercise, 4 R packages will be used:
The code block belows checks that the above packages are installed and loaded into the R working environment.
pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse)
The next code block imports the CSV file:
exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

Rows: 322 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
dbl (3): ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

title: Hands-On Exercise 2
text:
How does ggrepel work?
The code block below shows the implementation of ggrepel based on the scatter plot that was done above.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Hands-On Exercise 2
text:
How would ggtheme package work?
With the above example given, let’s explore the package. This pacakage provides themes that would replicate looks of plot by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
The following code example below follows the Economist theme is used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-On Exercise 2
text:
How does hrbthemes work?
Using the example given above, the code block below does 3 changes:

axis_title_size argument is used to increase the font size to 18
base_size argument is used to increase the default axis label to 15
grid argument is used to remove the x-axis grid lines

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")

title: Hands-On Exercise 2
section: Using the patchwork extension to create composite graphics
text:
Using the patchwork extension to create composite graphics
This extension is one of the ggplot2 extensions which is designed to combine separate ggplot2 graphs into single figure.
The general syntax that combines graphs are as follows:

2-column layout using the + sign
() to create a subplot group
2-row layout using the / sign


2 graphs combined
The code block below shows how 2 histograms are combined

p1 + p2










3 graphs combined
The code block below does the following to combine 3 plots:

/ stacks 2 ggplot2 graphs
| places the plots beside each other
() defines the sequence of the plotting


(p1 / p2) | p3

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Creating the composite figure with tags
The package also provides auto-tagging capabilities to identify the subplots in the figure with plot_annotation().

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Creating the composite figure with insert
The package also allows plots to be placed freely on top/below another plot with inset_element().

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Combining both patchwork and ggtheme while creating a composite figure
The code block showcases how both ggtheme and patchwork can be combined.

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
